description: Core UE5 gameplay classes: GameMode, GameState, PlayerController, PlayerState, Pawn, Character, HUD, and their lifecycle

entries.title: GameMode (AGameModeBase / AGameMode)
entries.description: The GameMode defines the rules of the game. It exists ONLY on the server (never on clients). It controls: what Pawn to spawn, what PlayerController to use, match state management, and player join/leave handling.
entries.whatItIs: The authoritative server-side class that governs game rules, player spawning, and match flow. It is the single source of truth for how the game operates and is never replicated to clients.
whenToUse: When you need to define which Pawn, PlayerController, HUD, GameState, and PlayerState classes to use
When implementing match flow logic (pre-match countdown, round transitions, match end conditions)
When you need to handle player join/leave events on the server
When you need to validate whether a player is allowed to join (PreLogin)
When implementing team assignment logic at spawn time
When you need to control spawn locations and respawn rules
whenNotToUse: When you need data accessible on clients — GameMode is server-only
When storing game state that clients need to see — use GameState instead
When handling player input — use PlayerController
When storing per-player replicated data — use PlayerState
For persistent data across map transitions — use GameInstance
commonMistakes: Trying to access GameMode on clients — it only exists on the server; use GameState for replicated data
Putting UI or input logic in GameMode — it has no concept of a local player
Forgetting to call Super:: on overridden functions like PostLogin, causing broken initialization chains
Using AGameMode when AGameModeBase would suffice — AGameMode adds match state overhead you may not need
Storing transient per-player data in GameMode instead of PlayerState
Not setting DefaultPawnClass and relying on engine defaults, leading to unexpected spawns
keyPoints: Only exists on the server — clients cannot access it directly
AGameModeBase: lightweight base. AGameMode: adds match state machine (WaitingToStart, InProgress, etc.)
Set per-map in World Settings or globally in Project Settings > Maps & Modes
Key functions: InitGame(), PreLogin(), PostLogin(), HandleStartingNewPlayer(), Logout()
DefaultPawnClass, PlayerControllerClass, HUDClass, GameStateClass, PlayerStateClass — all configurable
Use GetGameMode<T>() on the server to access it
entries.lifecycle: InitGame() → PreLogin() → Login() → PostLogin() → HandleStartingNewPlayer() → SpawnDefaultPawnFor()
entries.blueprintExample:
Blueprint Setup:
1. Create a new Blueprint Class with parent 'Game Mode Base'
2. Open the Blueprint and go to the Class Defaults panel
3. Set 'Default Pawn Class' to your character Blueprint (e.g. BP_MyCharacter)
4. Set 'Player Controller Class', 'Game State Class', 'Player State Class' as needed
5. To use this GameMode: go to World Settings in your level → set 'GameMode Override' to your BP_MyGameMode
6. For global default: Project Settings → Maps & Modes → Default GameMode
7. Override 'Event OnPostLogin' in the Event Graph to run logic when a player joins

entries.title: GameState (AGameStateBase / AGameState)
entries.description: The GameState holds replicated game-wide state that ALL players need to know about: score, match time, team data, etc. It exists on server and is replicated to all clients.
entries.whatItIs: A replicated actor that serves as the public billboard for game-wide data. It is the client-visible counterpart to GameMode, holding any shared state that every player needs to see such as scores, match phase, and team information.
whenToUse: When you need game-wide data visible to all players (scores, match timer, wave count)
When clients need to read the current match state or game phase
When you need a replicated list of all players via PlayerArray
When implementing a scoreboard or match summary screen
When tracking team-level data like team scores or team player counts
whenNotToUse: For per-player data — use PlayerState instead
For server-only game rules and logic — use GameMode
For private or sensitive player data that other clients should not see — use PlayerController or a custom actor with owner-only replication
For data that needs to persist across level transitions — use GameInstance
For transient local UI state that does not need replication
commonMistakes: Storing per-player data in GameState instead of PlayerState, causing unnecessary data coupling
Forgetting to add UPROPERTY(Replicated) and implement GetLifetimeReplicatedProps, so data never reaches clients
Using AGameState when AGameStateBase would suffice — AGameState adds match state replication you may not need
Modifying GameState on clients — authority lives on the server; client changes will be overwritten
Not using OnRep_ callbacks for client-side reactions to state changes, leading to polling in Tick
keyPoints: Replicated to all clients — use for shared game data
AGameStateBase: basic. AGameState: adds match state replication from AGameMode
PlayerArray contains all PlayerState objects — use to iterate over all players
Accessed via GetGameState<T>() from any actor
Good for: team scores, match timer, wave number, game phase
NOT for per-player data — use PlayerState for that

entries.title: PlayerController (APlayerController)
entries.description: The PlayerController represents the player's will — it receives input and possesses a Pawn. Each human player has exactly one PlayerController. It persists across Pawn deaths and level transitions.
entries.whatItIs: The non-physical representation of a human player. It bridges the gap between raw hardware input and in-world actions by possessing Pawns, managing the camera, and owning the HUD. It persists even when the player's Pawn is destroyed.
whenToUse: When you need to handle player input setup and input mode switching (Game, UI, or both)
When managing the camera through PlayerCameraManager
When creating or managing HUD and UMG widget display
When you need logic that persists across Pawn deaths (respawn timers, spectating)
When implementing pause menus, input mode toggling, or mouse cursor visibility
When you need to execute server RPCs that are tied to a specific player's authority
whenNotToUse: For AI-controlled entities — use AAIController instead
For storing data other players need to see — use PlayerState (PlayerController is not replicated to other clients)
For physics, movement, or visual representation — use the Pawn/Character
For game-wide rules — use GameMode
For data that must survive level transitions — use GameInstance (PlayerController is destroyed on non-seamless travel)
commonMistakes: Putting gameplay logic in PlayerController that belongs in the Pawn — the controller should control, not act
Assuming other clients have access to your PlayerController — only the server and the owning client have it
Forgetting to call Super::SetupInputComponent(), breaking the input chain
Not using Enhanced Input System in UE5 and relying on deprecated BindAction/BindAxis
Casting to a specific PlayerController class from other clients' code, which returns null because it does not exist on their machine
Handling input directly in PlayerController instead of forwarding to the Pawn via possession
keyPoints: One per human player — persists even when the Pawn dies
Owns the input system: receives raw input and routes it to the possessed Pawn
Manages the camera: PlayerCameraManager
Manages the HUD and UI widgets
In multiplayer: exists on server + owning client only (NOT on other clients)
Key functions: SetupInputComponent(), OnPossess(), OnUnPossess(), SetInputMode()
Use GetController<APlayerController>() from a Pawn, or GetWorld()->GetFirstPlayerController() for single-player
entries.lifecycle: Created at Login → OnPossess(Pawn) → Receives Input → OnUnPossess() → OnPossess(NewPawn)

entries.title: PlayerState (APlayerState)
entries.description: Stores per-player data that ALL players need to know about: name, score, team, ping. Replicated to everyone. Persists across Pawn deaths.
entries.whatItIs: A replicated actor that acts as the public data record for a single player. Every player in the game has one, and all clients can read every player's PlayerState, making it ideal for scoreboards, team indicators, and nameplates.
whenToUse: When storing per-player data that all other players need to see (score, kills, deaths, team)
When building a scoreboard or leaderboard that reads live player stats
When you need player identity info accessible by all clients (player name, unique ID, ping)
When tracking statistics that must survive Pawn death and respawn
When implementing team assignment that other players can query
whenNotToUse: For private per-player data like inventory contents or quest progress — use PlayerController or a custom actor with owner-only replication
For game-wide data like match timer or team totals — use GameState
For data that must persist across level transitions — use GameInstance or a SaveGame object
For heavy or complex data structures that would waste bandwidth replicating to every client
For AI bot data that does not need player-like replication
commonMistakes: Storing large amounts of data in PlayerState — it replicates to ALL clients, so keep it lean
Forgetting that PlayerState is automatically created for each PlayerController — do not create it manually
Accessing PlayerState before it is replicated on a client, leading to null pointer crashes
Using PlayerState for private data like inventory, which exposes it to all other clients
Not using OnRep_ functions for client-side updates, polling in Tick instead
Confusing PlayerState lifetime with Pawn lifetime — PlayerState survives Pawn death, but a new one is created on reconnect
keyPoints: Replicated to ALL clients — everyone can see every player's state
Persists across Pawn deaths and respawns
Automatically created for each PlayerController
Default properties: PlayerName, PlayerId, Ping, Score
Use for: player score, kill count, team assignment, player level/rank
Access from Pawn: GetPlayerState<AMyPlayerState>()
NOT for private data (inventory details, etc.) — use the PlayerController or a custom replicated actor

entries.title: Pawn and Character (APawn / ACharacter)
entries.description: A Pawn is the physical representation of a player or AI in the world. ACharacter extends APawn with a CharacterMovementComponent for built-in walking, jumping, flying, and swimming.
entries.whatItIs: The in-world physical avatar that a Controller possesses. APawn is the minimal base with collision and controller support, while ACharacter adds a capsule collider, skeletal mesh, and the powerful CharacterMovementComponent with built-in networked movement prediction.
whenToUse: Use ACharacter when you need humanoid movement with walking, jumping, crouching, swimming, or flying out of the box
Use APawn when you need a non-humanoid entity like a vehicle, drone, or turret with custom movement
When you need a physical entity in the world that can be possessed by a player or AI
When you need built-in network movement prediction and correction (ACharacter)
When you need to bind player input via SetupPlayerInputComponent
whenNotToUse: For non-physical game logic — use PlayerController, GameMode, or GameState
For simple projectiles or environmental objects — use AActor directly
If you need fully custom movement without CharacterMovementComponent overhead — use APawn with a custom MovementComponent
For AI logic and decision-making — use AIController; the Pawn is just the body
If you do not need possession semantics at all — use a plain AActor
commonMistakes: Using ACharacter for vehicles or flying drones — CharacterMovementComponent is designed for bipedal movement and adds unnecessary overhead
Placing gameplay logic in the Pawn that belongs in the Controller, coupling AI and player behavior together
Forgetting that SetupPlayerInputComponent is only called for the locally controlled Pawn — do not rely on it for server-side setup
Directly setting actor location for movement instead of using the MovementComponent, which breaks collision and network prediction
Not calling Super::SetupPlayerInputComponent(), breaking parent class input bindings
Assuming the Pawn always has a valid Controller — it can be unpossessed
keyPoints: APawn: base class. Has a Controller, can receive input. No built-in movement.
ACharacter: adds CapsuleComponent, CharacterMovementComponent, SkeletalMeshComponent
CharacterMovementComponent handles: walking, jumping, falling, flying, swimming, crouching, network prediction
Possessed by a Controller (PlayerController for humans, AIController for AI)
SetupPlayerInputComponent() is where you bind input for the local player
In multiplayer: the Pawn is replicated. Server spawns it, clients get a replicated copy
Destroyed on death — PlayerController survives and possesses a new Pawn on respawn
entries.blueprintExample:
Blueprint Setup:
1. Create a new Blueprint Class with parent 'Character'
2. The Blueprint automatically comes with: CapsuleComponent, ArrowComponent, Mesh (SkeletalMesh), and CharacterMovement
3. Select the 'CharacterMovement' component in the Components panel to configure:
   - Max Walk Speed (default 600)
   - Jump Z Velocity (default 420)
   - Can Crouch, Can Swim, Can Fly, etc.
4. To set up the mesh: select the 'Mesh' component → set Skeletal Mesh and Animation Blueprint
5. For input: in Event Graph, use 'Event SetupPlayerInputComponent' or use Enhanced Input Actions
6. To move: use 'Add Movement Input' node with 'Get Actor Forward Vector' for direction
7. To jump: use the built-in 'Jump' and 'Stop Jumping' functions — enable 'Can Jump' in CharacterMovement

entries.title: GameInstance (UGameInstance)
entries.description: The GameInstance persists for the entire lifetime of the game process. It is NOT destroyed during level transitions, making it ideal for data that must survive map changes.
entries.whatItIs: A UObject (not an Actor) that lives from engine initialization to application shutdown. It is the only standard framework class that truly persists across level loads and seamless travel, making it the go-to place for data and systems that must outlive any single map.
whenToUse: When you need data or state that survives level transitions (player profile, save system handle, audio manager)
When managing online subsystem sessions, matchmaking, or lobby state
When implementing a persistent save/load system manager
When tracking global settings like audio volume or language preference
When you need initialization logic that runs once at application startup (Init event)
whenNotToUse: For game-wide replicated state — use GameState (GameInstance is NOT replicated)
For per-level game rules — use GameMode
For per-player replicated data — use PlayerState
For anything that needs to be an Actor in the world with transforms and components
When the Subsystem pattern is more appropriate — consider UGameInstanceSubsystem for modular persistent features
commonMistakes: Assuming GameInstance is replicated — it is local to each machine; server and client each have their own
Using GameInstance as a dumping ground for everything — keep it focused; use Subsystems for modular features
Referencing world-specific Actors from GameInstance without null-checking — the world changes on level load
Forgetting to set the custom GameInstance class in Project Settings > Maps & Modes, so the default one is used
Storing large assets in GameInstance that never get unloaded, causing memory bloat
Not overriding Init() and Shutdown() for proper lifecycle management
keyPoints: Created when the game starts, destroyed when the game exits
Survives level loads and seamless travel — unlike all Actors
NOT replicated — each machine (server/client) has its own instance
Use for: persistent player data, save system management, analytics, global settings
Set in Project Settings > Maps & Modes > Game Instance Class
Access via GetGameInstance<UMyGameInstance>() from any UObject with a World

entries.title: Actor Lifecycle
entries.description: Understanding when Actor functions are called is critical. The lifecycle goes from construction through initialization, gameplay, and destruction.
entries.whatItIs: The ordered sequence of engine-called functions that every AActor goes through from creation to garbage collection. Mastering this lifecycle is essential for knowing where to safely initialize variables, bind delegates, start gameplay logic, and clean up resources.
whenToUse: When deciding where to place initialization code (Constructor vs BeginPlay vs PostInitializeComponents)
When you need to understand why a variable is null at a certain point in execution
When setting up or tearing down timers, delegates, and async operations
When debugging actors that behave differently in PIE vs packaged builds due to initialization order
When implementing object pooling and need to understand reactivation vs fresh construction
whenNotToUse: You always need to understand the lifecycle — there is no case where it does not apply to Actors
However, do not override every lifecycle function just in case — only override what you actually need
Do not use Constructor for gameplay logic — the world, other actors, and even the game mode may not exist yet
commonMistakes: Putting gameplay logic in the Constructor — it runs for the CDO (Class Default Object) and during editor time, not just at spawn
Using GetWorld(), GetGameMode(), or GetPlayerController() in the Constructor — the world does not exist yet for the CDO
Forgetting that BeginPlay order between actors is NOT guaranteed — do not assume another actor's BeginPlay has already run
Not cleaning up timers and delegates in EndPlay, causing crashes when the timer fires on a destroyed actor
Using BeginDestroy for gameplay cleanup — use EndPlay instead; BeginDestroy is for engine garbage collection
Calling Destroy() and expecting immediate destruction — the actor is marked pending kill and cleaned up later by GC
Not calling Super:: on overridden lifecycle functions, breaking the initialization chain
keyPoints: Constructor: called for CDO AND real instances. Only CreateDefaultSubobject here. No gameplay logic.
PostInitializeComponents(): called after all components are initialized. Good for caching component refs.
BeginPlay(): called when the actor is ready for gameplay. World is valid. Safe for gameplay setup.
Tick(DeltaTime): called every frame if ticking is enabled.
EndPlay(): called when the actor is being removed. Clean up timers, delegates, async tasks.
BeginDestroy()/FinishDestroy(): GC cleanup. Don't use for gameplay — use EndPlay.
entries.lifecycle: Constructor → PostInitializeComponents → BeginPlay → Tick (every frame) → EndPlay → BeginDestroy
entries.blueprintExample:
Blueprint Equivalents:
1. Constructor → 'Construction Script' (runs in editor and at spawn, use for setup that should preview in editor)
2. BeginPlay → 'Event BeginPlay' (the most common starting point — safe to access other actors and the world)
3. Tick → 'Event Tick' (called every frame — avoid heavy logic here; use Timers instead when possible)
4. EndPlay → 'Event EndPlay' (clean up references, stop timers)
5. To add a Timer instead of Tick: use 'Set Timer by Function Name' or 'Set Timer by Event' node
6. Key difference: Blueprint Construction Script runs in the editor for preview, C++ Constructor runs for the CDO too
